Red deer

[NO_DEER_001]

Authors
Affiliations

Aud H. Halbritter

University of Bergen

Erik Framstad

Norwegian Institute for Nature Research

Katrine Eldegard

Norwegian University of Life Sciences

Published

June 30, 2026

Reviewed by
Anders Kolstad

Recommended citation: Halbritter, A. H., Framstad, E., and Eldegard, K. 2026. Red deer [Hjorte] (ID(s): NO_DEER_001) v. 1.0.0. ecRxiv: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_DEER_001

Metadata
Log
  • May 15, 2026 - Initial draft for ecRxiv
  • June 30, 2026 - Accepted

1. Summary

  1. Background and rationale: The red deer (Cervus elaphus) is a large ungulate found in and around forest ecosystems in Norway. Through browsing and grazing, red deer shape vegetation composition, plant biodiversity and forest structure, and as a large prey animal also support populations of apex predators (Carranza et al. 2025; Mattioli et al. 2022). In the period with reasonably good overview of cervid densities in Norway (about the last 200 years), populations have been strongly influenced by human activity, including hunting, varying levels of control of large predators, and changes in land use and climate.

  2. Methods: The red deer variable expresses population density as number of individuals per km² below the climatic tree line, reported at county level. The data comes from the national monitoring program for cervids. Much of the monitoring material is collected from animals harvested during hunting. The reference condition is an expert-assessed state in which red deer in suitable forest habitat under current climate conditions, with natural, non-managed predator regimes, and in the absence of forest management. Estimated densities by county are normalised using a unimodal scaling function. Scaled county values are aggregated to five geographic regions and national level.

  3. Interpretation: An indicator value of 1 means that red deer abundance in the reporting unit is as large as expected under the reference condition. Values <1 indicate that the density is either lower or higher than the reference value.

  4. Results and development needs: For the current reporting period (2020-2024) the national indicator value is 0.35, with extremely low values for regions West (0.02) and North (0,04). The national indicator value has increased from 0.15 in 1990, with a small decline since 2019. Values have increased overall for most regions, except for a strong overall decline for region West and a moderate decline since 2019 for region South. There are no specific development needs, except for a possible review of reference values and the scaling function.

1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

  1. Bakgrunn og begrunnelse: Hjort (Cervus elaphus) er et stort hjortedyr som lever i og i tilknytning til skogsøkosystemer i Norge. Gjennom beiting former hjorten vegetasjonssammensetning, plantebiodiversitet og skogsstruktur, og som et stort byttedyr bidrar den også til å opprettholde bestander av toppredatorer (Carranza et al. 2025; Mattioli et al. 2022). I perioden med rimelig oversikt over hjortevilttettheter i Norge (ca. 200 år) har bestandene vært sterkt påvirket av menneskelig aktivitet, inkludert jakt, varierende grad av bekjempelse av store rovdyr, samt endringer i arealbruk og klima.

  2. Metoder: Variabelen for hjortebestand uttrykker tetthet som antall individer per km² under den klimatiske tregrensen, rapportert på fylkesnivå. Dataene kommer fra det nasjonale overvåkingsprogrammet for hjortevilt, med mye materiale fra jakt og statistikk via Hjorteviltregisteret. Referansetilstanden er en ekspertvurdert tilstand der hjort kan forekomme i egnete skogområder med dagens klima, med naturlige, ikke-regulerte rovdyrbestander, og uten skogforvaltning. Fylkesverdier normaliseres med en unimodal skaleringsfunksjon. Skalerte fylkesverdier aggregeres til fem geografiske regioner og nasjonalt nivå.

  3. Tolkning: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at hjortebestanden i rapporteringsenheten er like stor som forventet under referansetilstanden. Verdier <1 viser at tettheten enten er lavere eller høyere enn referanseverdien.

  4. Resultater og utviklingsbehov: For nåværende rapporteringsperiode (2020-2024) er den nasjonale indikatoren 0,35, men med ekstremt lave verdier for regionene Vest (0,02) og Nord (0,04). For hele landet har indikatorverdien økt fra 0,15 i 1990, men med en liten nedgang siden 2019. Indikatorverdien for de fleste regionene har også økt, men det har vært en sterk nedgang siden 1990 for region Vest og en moderat nedgang siden 2019 for region Sør. Det er ingen spesielle utviklingsbehov, med mulig unntak for en gjennomgang av referanseverdier og skaleringsfunksjonen.

2. About the underlying data

The red deer abundance data comes from the national cervid monitoring programme and Hjorteviltregisteret (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026; Landbruksdirektoratet 2026).

2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

The underlying data are collected annually. Indicator values are available for the assessment years in Naturindeksen: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024.

To produce indicator estimates for these years, input is typically combined over a short window (typically two to three years).

Spatially, data and indicator values are handled at county level, then aggregated to five geographic regions and national level for reporting.

2.2 Sampling design

The national monitoring program for cervids collects most material from harvested animals during hunting seasons (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026). For red deer, samples can include lower jaw and slaughter weight (Landbruksdirektoratet and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research 2026). Further methodological details are given on Naturindeksen – hjort.

2.3 Original units

The red deer abundance is expressed as number of individuals per km².

2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

The underlying data on hunting statistics are available upon request from Hjorteviltregisteret (Landbruksdirektoratet 2026). However, these data must be combined with data on demographic variables and other parameters to calculate the density of red deer per county, as described here. The resulting data for the density of red deer can be accessed directly and used in calculating scaled indicator values as described in sections 7 and 9 below.

Indicator values from the Norwegian Nature Index can be found in Nater, Zeiner-Henriksen, and Nybø (2025). Indicator values, variable values and reference values can be downloaded from the Nature Index database using the API, as I have done below. The data is licenced under CC-BY 4.0.

3. Indicator properties

3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

Large herbivores may potentially have a strong impact on the composition of the vegetation and the recruitment of tree species. Hence, this indicator is considered to fall under B3 Functional state characteristics.

3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

The ecosystem characteristic represented in this indicator is an ecosystem where species abundances and composition is similar to that in the reference condition.

3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

The indicator may covary with other cervid abundance indicators (moose, roe deer).

3.4 Impact factors

One of the major natural impact factors is variation in weather conditions, especially temperature during reproduction and snow conditions in winter, affecting food quality and availability, recruitment and survival. Predation from large carnivores and disease and parasites are other key factors affecting survival. Major anthropogenic impact factors are land use change, traffic accidents, hunting, strong limitation of large carnivore populations, and climate change.

3.5 Spatial aggregation

Spatial aggregation follows the following pathway: Aggregate (population model, county level) - [Scale - Truncate - Transform] - Aggregate (national level): population density estimates are available per county. Each county value is normalised with a unimodal scaling function (optimum at X100; lower and upper reference values for very deteriorated condition at X0 and X0h, respectively; see Section 5.2). Regional (east, south, west, central, north) and national indicator values are then obtained as area-weighted combinations of the scaled county values.

4. Reference condition and levels

4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition for red deer represents the natural population state occurring in the absence of significant human influence, where population size, structure and dynamics are regulated primarily by natural processes – including predation by natural carnivore communities, food availability, and habitat carrying capacity under natural vegetation conditions. The population fulfills its natural functional role as a large herbivore in forest ecosystems, influencing vegetation composition, plant biodiversity and forest structure through browsing and grazing at naturally occurring intensities, and contributing to nutrient cycling and the prey base of apex predators. Population size naturally varies within dynamic ranges in response to climatic variation, natural disturbance regimes, and ecological interactions with other herbivores such as moose and roe deer.

4.2 Reference levels

For this indicator, normalisation uses a unimodal scaling function. The optimum density in the reference condition corresponds to X100. The lower and upper reference values for the non-scaled variable in the worst possible condition (X0 and X0h) represent the scaling parameters, respectively, below and above the optimal density. County-specific values for these reference levels are defined in Naturindeksen; see Naturindeksen – hjort and the imported referenceValues object in Section 8.

4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

Reference levels and indicator values apply at county level. Aggregated results for five geographic regions and for Norway are area-weighted average indicator values for included counties. Normalisation should be done at county level before regional or national aggregation.

5. Uncertainties

5.1 General uncertainties

Reference levels are held fixed in this workflow (their own uncertainties are not propagated). Regional and national values are area-weighted average indicator values for included counties, so they inherit both model uncertainty at county level and any mismatch between county boundaries and ecological variation within regions.

5.2 Reported uncertainties

The intervals in Table 2, Table 3 and Figure 2 are inferential: they describe uncertainty around the estimated area-weighted mean indicator for each reporting unit (a geographic region or Norway as a whole), given the statistical models used for county-level density in Naturindeks.

For each county and assessment year, Naturindeks provides a predictive distribution for density (expressed as variable values before scaling). We draw many random values from that distribution per county, scale each draw to the 0–1 indicator scale using the county reference optimum and unimodal scaling, then build a large set of possible county indicator values. To obtain uncertainty for a region, we repeat the following 1000 times: draw one random county indicator value per county in that region, take the forest-area-weighted average across those counties, and record that regional mean. The median of those regional means is reported as the point estimate; the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles form an approximate 95% interval, and the 25% and 75% quantiles form an approximate interquartile range. For Norway, we apply a second Monte Carlo step at regional level: draw one value from each region’s uncertainty distribution, take the forest-area-weighted mean across the five regions, and repeat 1000 times to obtain a national distribution, then summarise in the same way. This approach reflects uncertainty in the county models and in the scaled values, while treating the regional or national figure as a single summary parameter rather than as a random draw from one county.

6. References

Carranza, J., S. Mattioli, R. Putman, F. J. Pérez-Barbería, and I. J. Gordon. 2025. “Red Deer Cervus Elaphus Linnaeus, 1758.” In Deer of the World, edited by M. Melletti and S. Focardi. Fascinating Life Sciences. Cham: Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-17756-9_10.
Landbruksdirektoratet. 2026. “Hjorteviltregisteret.” https://www.hjorteviltregisteret.no.
Landbruksdirektoratet, and Norwegian Institute for Nature Research. 2026. “Nasjonalt Overvåkingsprogram for Hjortevilt.” https://hjortevilt.no/forskning-og-viltovervaking/nasjonalt-bestandsovervakningsprogram-for-hjortevilt-bop.
Mattioli, S., F. E. Zachos, L. Rossi, A. M. Lister, and L. Corlatti. 2022. “Red Deer Cervus Elaphus Linnaeus, 1758.” In Terrestrial Cetartiodactyla, edited by L. Corlatti and F. E. Zachos. Handbook of the Mammals of Europe. Cham: Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-24475-0_19.
Nater, Chloé Rebecca, Tuva Zeiner-Henriksen, and Signe Nybø. 2025. “NI2025: Indicator Data.” https://doi.org/10.21345/y790-v762.
Norwegian Institute for Nature Research, and partners. 2025. “Norwegian Nature Index (Naturindeks).” https://www.naturindeks.no.

7. Import and prepare datasets

Run the download chunk once to refresh the local export. This project’s renv environment installs NIcalc from GitHub ChloeRN/NIcalc, branch include_ReadOnly_access (see renv.lock and install_deps.R); that build defaults to the Naturindeks read-only API user, so you do not need username / password arguments here. When NINAnor/NIcalc PR #59 is merged into NINAnor/NIcalc, you can switch the renv install back to the main repo and update the lockfile. We save the RDS export under data/ so that we do not need to download the data every time we run the analysis.

Download red deer data from Naturindeks API 
deerImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Hjort"),
  year = c("1990", "2000", "2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)

saveRDS(deerImport, here::here("data/deerImport.rds"))

8. Spatial units

According to the Naturindeks terminology, the Norwegian municipalities are BSunits, the 15 counties are ICunits, and the five geographic regions in Norway (east, south, west, central, north) are NI units. We can think of the counties as ecosystem assets, and mainland Norway as the Ecosystem accounting Area.

9. Analyses

This section uses the following workflow: we take variable values and reference levels from the Naturindeks import, normalise at county scale, then propagate uncertainty and aggregate spatially with area weights using ecTools::ec_upscale(). At regional and national scale, uncertainties are inferential: each replicate is an area-weighted mean based on one draw per county (then, for Norway, one draw per region), not a random draw from a pooled mixture of counties. We use unimodal scaling and forest area (Skog) as weights.

County-level abundance in the Naturindeksdatabase is stored as a predictive distribution (parameters or custom distributions), not only as a point estimate. Reference optimum density per county comes from the reference slice of the same download (expectedValue for the reference year). We ignore uncertainty in reference levels.

Assemble import and indicator object

assembleNiObject() reshapes deerImport so that indicator observations are in one list element per assessment year, and so that municipality-level BSunits (with forest area and NI region code part) are aligned with the thematic index structure used in Naturindeks.

Assemble red deer NI object (thematic, predefined regions) 
deerAssemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = deerImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)

Check input data ..... 

Elicitate incomplete reference values and indicator observations ..... 

Assemble dataset .....

Reference levels (X100)

Before scaling, we need the county-specific optimum on the variable scale (red deer density, ind. km⁻² below the tree line). These are read from referenceValues in the RDS export (rows with yearId == 0 or reference year name), one x100 per IC unit (county).

County reference optimum (X100) from downloaded Naturindeks data 
x100 <- deerImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
  dplyr::filter(.data$yearId == 0L | grepl("Referanse", .data$yearName, fixed = FALSE)) |>
  dplyr::distinct(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue)
x100 |>
  gt::gt()
County reference optimum (X100) by county (IC unit).
ICunitId ICunitName x100
3631 Akershus 1.0
3632 Hedmark 0.2
3633 Oppland 0.2
3634 Buskerud 0.2
3635 Vestfold 1.0
3636 Telemark 0.3
3637 Aust-Agder 0.3
3638 Vest-Agder 0.3
3639 Rogaland 1.0
3640 Hordaland 1.5
3641 Sogn og Fjordane 1.5
3642 Møre og Romsdal 1.5
3643 Sør-Trøndelag 1.0
3644 Nord-Trøndelag 0.3
3645 Nordland 0.2
6849 Østfold 1.0

Resample non-scaled county-level variables

For each assessment year, Naturindeks stores county values as a parameterised distribution (or a custom distribution). We draw nsim = 1000 random values per county with NIcalc::sampleObsMat(). Rows with a standard distribution family use observation type tradObs; rows with only a custom distribution use customObs.

Assessment years are the following: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024. The result county_draws is a long table of simulated variable values (value) by ICunitId and year (not yet on the 0–1 indicator scale).

Observation types for sampleObsMat 
myYears <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- vector("list", length(myYears))
for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  obs <- deerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName
  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"
  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}
Resample county-scale variables (non-scaled) 
county_draws <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = deerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = deerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = deerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = deerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = deerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = deerAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  colnames(myMat) <- paste0("s", seq_len(ncol(myMat)))
  myMat <- myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId"), names_to = "sim_col", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(
      sim_id = readr::parse_number(.data$sim_col),
      year = myYears[i]
    ) |>
    select(ICunitId, sim_id, value, year)

  county_draws <- county_draws |>
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(county_draws) |>
  gt::gt()
Preview of resampled county-scale variable values (first rows).
ICunitId sim_id value year
3631 1 0.008942646 1990
3631 2 0.006760789 1990
3631 3 0.005141639 1990
3631 4 0.002417333 1990
3631 5 0.002481014 1990
3631 6 0.006479741 1990

Scaling to indicator values (unimodal)

Normalisation is done at county scale before any regional or national aggregation. Each draw is joined to its county x100, then mapped to [0, 1] with ecTools::ec_normalise() in two-sided unimodal mode: x0 = 0 and x100 = optimum from the download define the best condition; x0h = 3 * x100 is a simple upper anchor for densities above the optimum. The sigmoid option applies a non-linear transform after the piecewise linear mapping.

Join X100 and unimodal scaling to indicator scale 
county_draws <- county_draws |>
  left_join(x100 |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)), by = join_by(ICunitId))

ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = county_draws$value,
  x0 = 0,
  x100 = county_draws$x100,
  x0h = 3 * county_draws$x100,
  fun = "sigmoid"
)

county_draws <- county_draws |> add_column(indicator = ind)

The scatter plot below (Figure 1) shows the mapping from variable to indicator for each county (one facet per ICunitName). The figure is saved when the document is rendered.

Save normalisation diagnostic figure (English) 
red_deer_plot <- ec_norm_plot(county_draws$value, county_draws$indicator) +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ county_draws$ICunitName, scales = "free_x") +
  ggplot2::theme(axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9))
red_deer_plot$layers[[1]]$aes_params$size <- 3
suppressMessages(
  ggsave(filename = here::here("img/deer_norm_plot_eng.png"), plot = red_deer_plot, scale = 1.5)
)
Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/deer_norm_plot_eng.png"))

Aggregation weights (forest area by county)

Spatial aggregation uses area below the tree line recorded in Naturindeks (Skog on each municipality in BSunits). Municipalities are linked to counties via ICunits, then forest area is summed to county (ICunitId) and the NI region part (N, C, W, S, E) is kept for the next step. Each row in county_draws then carries forest_area and part for weighting.

Forest area by county and NI region for aggregation weights 
county_weights <- as_tibble(deerAssemble$BSunits) |>
  rename(BSunitId = id)

IC_BC_link <- deerImport$ICunits |>
  select(ICunitId = id, BSunitId)

county_weights <- county_weights |>
  left_join(IC_BC_link, by = join_by(BSunitId))

county_weights <- county_weights |>
  group_by(ICunitId) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(Skog), part = unique(part)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))

county_draws <- county_draws |>
  left_join(county_weights, by = join_by(ICunitId))

Spatial aggregation

Aggregate from counties to geographic regions with ecTools::ec_upscale(), using Monte Carlo propagation of inferential uncertainty (one draw per county per replicate, forest-area-weighted mean).

County to region MC propagation (ec_upscale) 
mc_propagated <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = county_draws,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = forest_area,
  start_units = ICunitId,
  end_units = part,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated, here::here("data/deer_mc_propagated_regional.rds"))

For the national indicator, propagate again from regions to Norway. Summed forest area per geographic region is used as weight at this step.

Total forest area per NI region 
region_weights <- county_weights |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(.data$forest_area), .groups = "drop")
region_weights
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  part  forest_area
  <chr>       <dbl>
1 C          20568.
2 E          39436.
3 N          10896.
4 S          18229.
5 W          11479.
Region to Norway MC propagation (ec_upscale) 
mc_propagated_for_national <- mc_propagated |>
  left_join(region_weights, by = join_by(part)) |>
  add_column(end_units = "Norway")

mc_propagated_national <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = mc_propagated_for_national,
  variable = sampled_mean,
  weight = forest_area,
  start_units = part,
  end_units = end_units,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated_national, here::here("data/deer_mc_propagated_national.rds"))

Regional and national MC draws are combined for summarisation.

Bind regional and national MC outputs 
mc_propagated_combined <- mc_propagated |>
  select(year, part, sampled_mean) |>
  bind_rows(mc_propagated_national)

10. Results

From the 1000 Monte Carlo replicates per region and year in mc_propagated_combined, we report the median as the point estimate and use the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval for the area-weighted mean indicator (inferential uncertainty, not descriptive between-county spread). The tables (Table 2 and Table 3) and time series (Figure 2) summarise regional and national (Norway) values.

Code 
summary_out <- mc_propagated_combined |>
  mutate(part = dplyr::recode(part, !!!deer_part_labels)) |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(sampled_mean), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.025), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.25), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.75), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.975), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(part = factor(part, levels = deer_region_levels)) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B3",
    Indicator = "Red deer abundance",
    Ecosystem = "Boreal/montane forests",
    ID = "NO_DEER_001"
  )
Code 
deer_plot_years <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
deer_region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(deer_region_levels)),
  deer_region_levels
)

plot_data <- summary_out |>
  mutate(
    year = factor(year, levels = deer_plot_years),
    series_type = if_else(part == "Norway", "National", "Region")
  )

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part, group = part)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8,
    linewidth = 0.45,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = deer_region_cols, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(National = 19, Region = 17),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c(National = 1, Region = 0.55),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)),
    shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")
Code 
summary_out |>
  filter(part != "Norway") |>
  arrange(part, year) |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()
Code 
summary_out |>
  filter(part == "Norway") |>
  arrange(part, year) |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()

11. Export file

Regional and national Monte Carlo draws are exported in one table (mc_propagated_combined: year, part, sampled_mean; part includes NI regions and Norway).

Export regional and national MC-propagated indicator draws 
write.csv(
  mc_propagated_combined,
  here::here("data/results_NO_DEER_001_v1.0.0.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

12. Author contributions

CRediT statement

  • Conceptualization: [All]
  • Methodology: [AHH]
  • Formal analysis: [AHH]
  • Writing – original draft: [AHH]
  • Writing – review & editing: [All]

We would, also, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on Naturindeks (Norwegian Institute for Nature Research and partners 2025).

Session Info

This workflow uses renv. Run renv::restore() to recreate the working environment.
